locations.name: Gunung Padang
locations.blurb: A terraced megalithic hill in West Java, blanketed in columnar basalt stones arranged across five stepped terraces. Officially a megalithic site of perhaps 2,500 years, it sits atop a much older natural-and-modified hill.
locations.fun_fact: A controversial 2023 paper claimed buried layers could be 25,000+ years old — it was later retracted by the journal, fuelling exactly the debate this series thrives on.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock argues Gunung Padang may be the world's oldest pyramid — a man-made structure begun deep in the last Ice Age by a lost advanced culture, with the visible terraces just the cap of something far older underneath.

locations.name: Nan Madol
locations.blurb: A city of nearly 100 artificial islets built from colossal basalt logs in a lagoon off Pohnpei — the only ancient city ever constructed atop a coral reef. Its megalithic walls rise straight out of the Pacific.
locations.fun_fact: Some basalt 'logs' weigh up to 50 tonnes; how a small island society quarried, floated and stacked them remains genuinely unexplained.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock points to Nan Madol as evidence of lost engineering knowledge in the Pacific — Ice-Age-era memory of building with stones too heavy for the means we assume the builders had.

locations.name: Great Pyramid of Cholula
locations.blurb: By volume the largest pyramid (and largest monument) ever built on Earth — a grass-covered mountain in Cholula with a church on top and miles of tunnels beneath. Built in stages over many centuries.
locations.fun_fact: It's so overgrown the Spanish built a church on its summit in 1594 without realising the whole hill was a pyramid.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock uses Cholula to argue that Mesoamerica's pyramid-building tradition descends from a far older source — knowledge inherited, not invented from scratch.

locations.name: Xochicalco
locations.blurb: A hilltop city in Morelos with a famous observatory cave: a shaft cut so that, on the days the sun passes directly overhead, a beam of light strikes the chamber floor. A UNESCO World Heritage Site.
locations.fun_fact: Its 'Pyramid of the Feathered Serpent' carries a carved date thought to record astronomers correcting their calendar.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock highlights Xochicalco's precision sun-shaft as inherited astronomical science — the kind of exact sky-knowledge he believes was passed down from an earlier lost civilization.

locations.name: Tetzcotzinco
locations.blurb: A sculpted hill near Texcoco — terraces, baths and shrines carved straight into living rock and fed by a long engineered aqueduct. Often called the oldest botanical garden in the Americas.
locations.fun_fact: Water was carried for kilometres along a hand-cut channel just to fill royal pools carved into the bedrock.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock treats the rock-cut hydraulics here as another flash of advanced engineering surfacing in the archaeological record long before we'd expect it.

locations.name: Ġgantija Temples
locations.blurb: On the island of Gozo stand the Ġgantija temples — older than Stonehenge and the Pyramids of Giza, among the oldest free-standing stone buildings on Earth. Maltese legend said giants raised them.
locations.fun_fact: 'Ġgantija' literally means 'giant tower' — built from limestone blocks over five metres long around 3600 BCE.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock sees Malta's megalithic temples — and their astronomical alignments — as fingerprints of a single lost mother-culture, and suspects they are older than the official dates allow.

locations.name: Ħal Saflieni Hypogeum
locations.blurb: A three-level underground complex carved out of solid rock beneath Malta — a subterranean necropolis where the remains of some 7,000 people were found. One chamber, the 'Oracle Room', has uncanny acoustic resonance.
locations.fun_fact: Speak in the Oracle Room at the right pitch and your voice booms through the entire structure — a deliberate sonic effect cut into the stone.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock is fascinated by the Hypogeum's engineered acoustics, arguing such sophisticated sound design hints at knowledge inherited from a much earlier age.

locations.name: Bimini Road
locations.blurb: An underwater line of rectangular limestone blocks running half a mile along the seabed off North Bimini, in about 5 metres of water. Geologists call it natural beach rock; others see a drowned ancient road or wall.
locations.fun_fact: Its 'discovery' in 1968 fed the long-running myth that this is the road to Atlantis predicted by psychic Edgar Cayce.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock dives the site to argue it could be a man-made structure submerged by the meltwater sea-level rise at the end of the Ice Age — exactly where a lost coastal civilization's traces would now lie.

locations.name: Göbekli Tepe
locations.blurb: The oldest known megalithic temple on Earth — massive carved T-shaped pillars raised around 9600 BCE, some 6,000 years before Stonehenge. Built, astonishingly, by people we'd call hunter-gatherers.
locations.fun_fact: It was deliberately buried in antiquity, which is why it survived so intact — a 12,000-year-old time capsule.
locations.aa_claim: Göbekli Tepe is Hancock's keystone: he argues 'simple' foragers couldn't have done this alone, and that 'sages' — survivors of a lost Ice-Age civilization — seeded the knowledge here.

locations.name: Karahan Tepe
locations.blurb: Göbekli Tepe's sister site, 35 km east — part of the 'Taş Tepeler' cluster. Its standout chamber has rows of phallic pillars carved from bedrock and a watching serpent-necked head emerging from the wall.
locations.fun_fact: Excavations only ramped up in 2019, so much of Karahan Tepe is still literally coming out of the ground.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock cites Karahan Tepe as proof Göbekli Tepe wasn't a one-off — a whole region of advanced megalithic building appearing fully-formed right at the dawn of the Neolithic.

locations.name: Poverty Point
locations.blurb: A 3,400-year-old complex of vast concentric earthen ridges and mounds in northeast Louisiana, raised by hunter-gatherers. A UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of North America's great earthwork achievements.
locations.fun_fact: Mound A holds roughly 238,000 cubic metres of earth — moved basket by basket, apparently in a matter of months.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock argues the geometric precision and astronomical layout of Poverty Point imply inherited planning knowledge — not something he believes 'simple' foragers improvised.

locations.name: Serpent Mound
locations.blurb: The largest serpent effigy on Earth — a 411-metre snake of earth coiling along a ridge in Ohio, its open jaws aligned to the summer-solstice sunset. Built near the rim of an ancient meteorite impact crater.
locations.fun_fact: Its head lines up with the solstice sunset and its coils may track lunar events — a calendar written in soil.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock reads the Serpent Mound's astronomy — and its position over a cosmic-impact crater — as a deliberate memorial to a sky-borne catastrophe: the Ancient Apocalypse itself.

locations.name: Derinkuyu Underground City
locations.blurb: An entire city carved straight down into Cappadocia's soft volcanic rock — at least 18 levels reaching ~85 m deep, with room for thousands of people plus livestock, ventilation shafts and great rolling stone doors.
locations.fun_fact: Its air shafts still work: even at the deepest levels the air stays fresh, thanks to ventilation engineered into the rock.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock suggests these vast underground cities were originally dug as shelters — 'bunkers' — against a remembered threat from the sky, refuges for surviving a cataclysm.

locations.name: Kaymaklı Underground City
locations.blurb: The widest of Cappadocia's underground cities, a warren of low tunnels, stables, kitchens and churches on multiple subterranean levels — connected, by legend, to Derinkuyu several kilometres away.
locations.fun_fact: Tunnels are deliberately narrow and low, forcing any intruder to stoop single-file into the defenders' reach.
locations.aa_claim: Like Derinkuyu, Hancock frames Kaymaklı as part of a network of deep refuges — physical evidence, he argues, of a civilization that knew it needed to hide underground.

locations.name: Channeled Scablands
locations.blurb: A vast scarred landscape across eastern Washington gouged by apocalyptic Ice-Age megafloods. Dry Falls was once a waterfall five times the width of Niagara — now a silent cliff over an empty canyon.
locations.fun_fact: Geologists mocked the megaflood theory for decades before admitting J Harlen Bretz had been right all along — floods really did do this, almost overnight.
locations.aa_claim: The Scablands are Hancock's proof-of-concept for sudden catastrophe: cataclysmic floods at the end of the Ice Age that, he argues, could have wiped a coastal civilization off the map and out of memory.

locations.name: Murray Springs Clovis Site
locations.blurb: A Clovis-era hunting camp in Arizona's San Pedro Valley, where mammoth and bison kills lie sealed beneath a thin dark layer — the 'black mat' — that caps the bones around 12,800 years ago.
locations.fun_fact: That black mat marks the moment the Clovis people and the megafauna vanish from the record together — abruptly.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock points to the 'black mat' and its impact-spherules as physical residue of the Younger Dryas comet impact — the trigger, in his telling, of the Ancient Apocalypse.

locations.name: White Sands Footprints
locations.blurb: On the gypsum flats of White Sands, fossilised human footprints were dated to around 21,000–23,000 years ago — potentially the oldest firm evidence of people in the Americas, thousands of years before 'Clovis first'.
locations.fun_fact: Some trackways show an adult carrying a toddler, setting it down, then picking it up again — a 22,000-year-old moment frozen in mud.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock seizes on the White Sands dates as a wrecking ball to the textbook timeline — if people were here 23,000 years ago, there was ample time, he argues, for a now-lost civilization to rise and fall.

locations.name: Amazon Geoglyphs
locations.blurb: Across the Brazilian state of Acre, deforestation has revealed hundreds of giant geometric earthworks — perfect circles, squares and avenues — hidden under the Amazon for centuries. Evidence the 'untouched' rainforest was once heavily engineered.
locations.fun_fact: Many enclosures are near-perfect geometric shapes hundreds of metres across, only visible once the forest is cleared or seen from the air.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock uses the Amazon's hidden geoglyphs to argue the rainforest concealed a sophisticated lost world — proof, he says, that whole civilizations can vanish completely from view.

locations.name: Caverna da Pedra Pintada
locations.blurb: The 'Painted Rock Cave' near Monte Alegre in the Amazon, its walls covered in red ochre figures — handprints, animals and star-like shapes — painted some 11,000–13,000 years ago. Among the oldest rock art in the Americas.
locations.fun_fact: Some scholars read clusters of the painted dots as records of the night sky — a possible Ice-Age star map.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock interprets the cave's ancient art as encoded sky-knowledge, arguing these painters inherited an astronomical tradition far older than the images themselves.

locations.name: Rapa Nui (Easter Island)
locations.blurb: The most remote inhabited island on Earth, studded with nearly 1,000 moai — colossal stone heads-and-torsos carved at the Rano Raraku quarry and somehow moved miles to coastal platforms.
locations.fun_fact: The famous 'heads' have full buried bodies — and Rapa Nui tradition says the moai 'walked' to their platforms.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock points to Rapa Nui's megalithic skill and oral memory of moving giant stones as another echo, he argues, of engineering knowledge older than the island's settlement.

locations.name: Paracas Candelabra
locations.blurb: A 180-metre trident-shaped geoglyph carved into a sloping hillside on the Paracas Peninsula, facing the Pacific. Cut up to a metre deep into the hardened sand, it's been visible to ships for centuries.
locations.fun_fact: Pottery found nearby dates the figure to at least 200 BCE, but nobody knows for certain who made it — or for whom.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock links coastal geoglyphs like the Candelabra to a deep tradition of giant ground-markings, arguing they preserve symbols handed down from a much older culture.

locations.name: Sacsayhuamán
locations.blurb: A citadel above Cusco built from limestone blocks so vast — some over 100 tonnes — and so precisely fitted that a sheet of paper won't slide between them. No mortar, yet earthquake-proof for centuries.
locations.fun_fact: The largest stones weigh well over 100 tonnes and were shaped to interlock in jigsaw-perfect, many-angled joints.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock argues Sacsayhuamán's polygonal megalithic masonry may pre-date the Inca entirely — the legacy of a far older engineering culture the Inca later built upon.

locations.name: Pueblo Bonito, Chaco Canyon
locations.blurb: The heart of Chaco Canyon: 'great houses' like Pueblo Bonito with hundreds of rooms, laid out to the cardinal directions and aligned to the sun and moon. The hub of a society linked by hundreds of miles of dead-straight roads.
locations.fun_fact: Chaco's buildings track an 18.6-year lunar cycle — long-term sky-watching baked into the architecture.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock reads Chaco's astronomy and geometry as inherited science — precise sky-knowledge that, he argues, points back to a much older source than the Ancestral Puebloans alone.

locations.name: Palenque
locations.blurb: A jewel of Maya architecture rising out of the Chiapas jungle, famous for the tomb of king Pakal beneath the Temple of the Inscriptions — its carved sarcophagus lid one of the most debated images in archaeology.
locations.fun_fact: Pakal's lid shows the king poised over the jaws of the underworld — read by mainstream Mayanists as a rebirth scene, and by fringe writers as everything from a 'spaceman' to a tree of life.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock uses Palenque's iconography and astronomy to argue Maya cosmology preserves deep-time memory — knowledge, he believes, inherited from an earlier lost civilization.

locations.name: Chichén Itzá
locations.blurb: The great Maya-Toltec city of the Yucatán, crowned by El Castillo — a step-pyramid that is really a stone calendar, its 365 steps and precise geometry tuned to the solar year.
locations.fun_fact: At each equinox the afternoon sun casts a serpent of light and shadow that appears to slither down the pyramid's staircase.
locations.aa_claim: Hancock treats El Castillo's equinox serpent and calendar-precision as the high-water mark of inherited Mesoamerican astronomy — sky-science he traces back to the survivors of the Ancient Apocalypse.
